event: Neolithic farmers from Sicily first settle the Maltese islands, marking the beginning of permanent human habitation.
Construction begins on Malta's megalithic temples, among the oldest free-standing stone structures in the world.
The underground Hal Saflieni Hypogeum is hewn from rock, serving as a prehistoric sanctuary and necropolis.
The temple-building civilization mysteriously collapses, and a new Bronze Age culture replaces it.
Phoenician traders establish settlements, integrating Malta into Mediterranean maritime commerce.
Carthage takes control of Malta, fortifying the islands as part of its western Mediterranean empire.
Rome seizes Malta from Carthage during the Second Punic War, beginning centuries of Roman rule.
The Apostle Paul is shipwrecked on Malta, an event traditionally credited with bringing Christianity to the islands.
Malta is incorporated into the Byzantine Empire following Belisarius's reconquest of the central Mediterranean.
Arab forces from Aghlabid Ifriqiya conquer Malta, profoundly shaping its language and agriculture.
Count Roger I of Sicily captures Malta, bringing the islands under Norman rule.
Roger II of Sicily reasserts firm Norman control, accelerating the islands' re-Christianization and ties to Sicily.
Malta is drawn into the Crown of Aragon after the War of the Sicilian Vespers.
Emperor Charles V grants Malta to the Knights Hospitaller, who make it the seat of their order.
The Knights of St. John repel a massive Ottoman invasion in the Great Siege of Malta.
The fortified capital city of Valletta is founded and named after Grand Master Jean de Valette.
Napoleon Bonaparte captures Malta, ending the rule of the Knights of St. John.
Maltese insurgents and the British force the French garrison to surrender, placing Malta under British protection.
The Treaty of Paris formally confirms Malta as a British Crown Colony.
The opening of the Suez Canal boosts Malta's strategic importance as a Mediterranean naval coaling station.
Malta's first railway opens, linking Valletta with the inland town of Mdina.
British troops fire on protesters during the Sette Giugno riots, sparked by high bread prices and demands for self-government.
Malta is granted its first self-government constitution with a locally elected legislative assembly.
Maltese is recognized as an official language alongside English, displacing Italian.
Italy begins bombing Malta as the island becomes a besieged Allied stronghold in World War II.
King George VI awards the George Cross to the entire population of Malta for its wartime endurance.
Malta serves as a launching base for the Allied invasion of Sicily, and the Italian fleet surrenders in its waters.
Self-government is restored and women gain the right to vote in Maltese elections.
Maltese voters approve a referendum to integrate the colony more closely with the United Kingdom, though the plan ultimately fails.
Malta gains independence from Britain, becoming a sovereign state within the Commonwealth and joining the United Nations.
Dom Mintoff becomes prime minister and begins renegotiating defence agreements and rents with Britain and NATO.
Malta becomes a republic, replacing the British monarch with a Maltese president as head of state.
The last British military forces withdraw, ending Malta's role as a foreign naval base, celebrated as Freedom Day.
Malta's megalithic temples and the Hal Saflieni Hypogeum are inscribed as UNESCO World Heritage Sites.
The historic Bush-Gorbachev summit marking the end of the Cold War is held aboard ships off Malta's coast.
Malta accedes to the European Union as one of its smallest member states.
Malta adopts the euro as its official currency, replacing the Maltese lira.
A national referendum approves the introduction of divorce, ending Malta's status as one of the last countries to ban it.
Investigative journalist Daphne Caruana Galizia is assassinated by car bomb, triggering a major political crisis.
Valletta serves as a European Capital of Culture, showcasing the city's Baroque heritage.
Prime Minister Joseph Muscat resigns amid fallout from the Caruana Galizia murder investigation.
